speaker: interviewer
text: [? Arrestable. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And you want to know about the organization build up. Well, the organization was just the same as what you find in a township. Everything was there. There were offices for the kitchen, for the [? bath. ?] What we had for the-- then we had, of course, joinery. We had a laundry. We had-- well, every organization, [? say ?] what normally in a community exists. Next to it, [? campus ?] work was built in part of the municipality of Westerbork, where you could actually say it was a sort of no man's land. There was nothing [? as ?] sand, barren, completely. There was nothing at all. There was no electricity. There was no-- nothing of the kind. No sewer, and no town water. So we had to depend there on [INAUDIBLE] for the water. We had our own electricity provisions, and we made our own sewer, and had purification basin there outside for the sewer. And that water was used again for the-- we had a farm outside there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence around the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at that time. The camp was actually built by the Jewish organization, the [NON-ENGLISH], and their officers of the Department of Justice. And, well, the outlook of the camp was quite friendly in that time. There were barracks, but not barracks in the sense of concentration camp. Certainly not. It was all open. The only thing that I knew, that it was first built for the German Jewish people who came illegally over the border. And well, they were kept in-- in the first instance, they were kept all over the places, in Holland, different sorts of places. I don't know actually the number, but there were 30, 40 places where they were kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they concentrated them in, and that's where that started off in the beginning of [? '94, ?] to build their camp. First they were thinking about in the middle of Holland to build the camp, but these people weren't interested at all. And ultimately, they got Westerbork that they were so far interested, as long as they didn't have any community with it. And that's where they built it, just on the border actually, of the municipality. And-- So when you came to Westerbork, how many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in that time approximately 2,000 to 3,000. In the first instance, when they-- after-- the German-- when they started to build the camp, they, they brought over a couple of Jewish people from Germany, the Jewish people, and helped to build the-- finish off the buildings of the camp. So these were the first ones. And I think at that time, there were approximately 400. 400, 600, something like that, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the camp in early-- in October 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to stay in the camp up to that time. I couldn't leave. And then on the 2nd of October, I got the message that I was completely free to go where I wanted to. And so we took the first opportunity to have an outing ourselves too. And that was on the 3rd of October, and we went over to [? Hronia ?] to do some shopping over there, because we couldn't have done anything in the six weeks at all. We were fed in the camp. We didn't-- we couldn't cook. Everything was done in the camp. It wasn't-- well, you could say, not the same sort of [INAUDIBLE] about the [? German ?] [INAUDIBLE]. But there was an-- [? arrested ?] OK not to stay in the camp anyhow. And so the first opportunity was on the 3rd of October. We went over to [? Hronia. ?] And that's where we saw a hopeless situation there, because we were automatically-- we knew automatically they were Jewish people because of the yellow [? sign. ?] And I said, well, to my wife, I don't know what's going on, but something is going to happen anyhow because the station was full. And oh well, we stayed the whole day in [? Hronia, ?] and came back late in the afternoon. And then we saw it approaching the station in from Westerbork was [? Hoholland. ?] And from there on, all the people arrived there and had to walk down about four and a half to 5 kilometers to the camp. [? Well, ?] they were all packed. And well, these people couldn't carry all these things, so they left their baggage on the roadside. And from the camp later on, that was collected. But gee, that was a mess, a complete mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would there have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one day, that's what I got to know afterwards, the camp commander got the message from the German authorities that they were going to bring in slack-- that was the word, slack-- so all at once, approximately 16,000 people. And it happened that [? today. ?] So that evening, we had approximately 20,000 people in the camp, for a capacity of, at that time, not more than 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people couldn't get their accommodation. They couldn't be fed, because the kitchen couldn't cope with the situation at all. That was very bad. And the next day people were waiting even outside. And I remember that particular transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was still one coming in, and that had to wait outside because they was-- they were going to move out and another transport for Polish straight away. And they couldn't make up the numbers for that train. The Germans were actually only working in numbers. We want the transport of 1,000 people, and that's the number we want. And we couldn't care less what these people are, how these people are. If they are single, married, invalids, kids, doesn't matter, as long as we get our thousand. And there were Germans around for-- looking after the train that everything or everybody was in-- counted. And that is what they [? double ?] counted actually. And they were short of people. And then suddenly, one of the German's commander, that-- the transport, who wasn't even in the camp, who wasn't registered at that particular time. OK, out. These people are going into the train to make up the numbers. And that's where everybody got, well, reluctant to do anything. And just people started crying, and they saw their own family already in the camp. And they were waiting outside to come into the camp, and now they were moved straightaway already into a transport train. Oh, it was bad situation. Very bad. I said, well, if this goes on that way, it becomes hopeless. But that was only that particular day. And thereafter, things settled down in a more normal, organized way. But I will always remember that particular transport, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on, who was on that transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Was it men, and women, and children, or-- Mix, a complete mix. That's what I said. Elderly people, young people, babies, you name it. It's just a matter of numbers. That's what they wanted. What was the procedure when people come into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The normal procedure was always that they went first to the-- and we had our big hall, and that's where the people were registered and put on cards, full name, address, all the same sort of business what you're doing at registrar. All particulars were noted. If the whole family was together or people [? were ?] missing of the family and so on, that was all mentioned on the cards. And that's what I wanted to know too because in one instance, I tried everything to sabotage where possible. And when I started to work, I had to look after some people, and I got also messages from outside. Take care, there are coming in these people by that name. Take care that they are not go on transport, and so on. Or try to get them out of the camp. I got messages, even from London, to look after certain important people that they had to-- if they were coming in, then to bring them, straight away, outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the methods was-- firstly I just-- my office was on the outside of the camp. And I just called these people in, locked them in the office. And as soon as it was dark, they jumped out of the window. And they were outside and went down the road to the addresses where we gave them to, where they had to go to for the first contact. And afterwards then-- What was the people's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't want to know, and I don't want to know them now at all. I know some, of course. But most of them, I, straight away, locked them out of my mind, these names. Because if I was picked up and then I had to tell them or was forced to tell whom I have helped out of the camp, that I didn't know. So I locked them out of my mind all together. And the second method was to tear up the registration cards. I had complete entrance in all the offices because of my appointment there as the food distribution, so that they control and could do what the office asked of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tear them up. And then they were not known in the camp anymore, so they couldn't put on transport, until the commander got to know that there were more people in the camp than actually were on the register.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I followed one transport again. In this way, that he collected out of the camp quite a number of people, put them into one barrack, re-register through again, put them on list. And straightaway, into the train. That's what happened there once. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those people that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was forbidden to come into the registration office anymore. They couldn't prove that I was doing it, but they had an idea. That was just the fun of it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they--

speaker: interviewee
text: To do things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Germans. That was after January, 1943. That's why they took over already. Kept us [? coal. ?] Left in the beginning of January. And Gemmeker, the German commander, moved in at the end of January and took over the whole camp. And he-- a circular was given into the camp in which it was mentioned that from the 1st of January on, the camp was not any longer known as Camp Westerbork, but as the lager, lager Westerbork, but they had a third name. Durchgangslager Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Transit camp, for, Transit Camp Westerbork. That was the name. And from there on, it was not anymore the German commander and a Dutch commander. It was Lagerkommandant, Lagerkommandant Gemmeker. That we came to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the whole outside situation changed, and there was no Dutch government influence anymore. But they couldn't touch me. I was a separate, from a separate organization. I'm from a separate department. So all the other employees, which working under the Department of Justice by Captain Schol, these people were asked if they wanted to stay. And if they wanted to stay, then they got a further appointment from the SD, the German Sicherheitsdienst. So they were no longer employed by the Dutch government, but by German government.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why I said they couldn't touch me, because I was in a separate office all together. And they have to accept me because I was taking care about the food in the camp. And that's what they never could take of me. That's why I had the whole controlling business in the camp. I could go to any office in the camp, in any part of the camp, and check if the food was distributed according to the rules I had given.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rules?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the rules were, of course, that I gave them the same sort of food what was given to the people outside the camp. That was the first. And I gave it on a daily basis, not on coupons anymore. I issued the coupons on a daily production from the office. If it was stated then there were so many people in the camp. Out of these people, were so many in hospitals, so many in-- working in hard labor outside the camp because of the farm we had. And some people who were working in industries, which were handling poisonous materials, such as batteries, and airport-- airplane material from shot down airplanes that they were also sorted on metal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all these extras I had to give to those people too. But I didn't have to check what was given daily to everyone but most of it I had to look after those people who got the extras.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these were pregnant women. They got an extra feed for the day. They got extra milk. They got extra rations all together. And for the hospital, especially for the dying persons, these extras were given.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- that's where I did all my checks. So I went over to the hospital. Have these people got everything that they-- and that's one of the advantages of Westerbork, or had been. And that's what I'm very proud of. These people never, never had any form of hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah, you could say the rations were at the later period of the war far less than what we usually needed, but they got it and was never hungry. Even that was so that by every barrack, there was a tin or a canister, bin. In this I could drop the food, what they didn't like, or that had-- and that went over to the farm where we had 120 pigs and we fed all of them. [NON-ENGLISH] was on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for-- only for food, leftovers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there leftovers? [SCOFFING]

speaker: interviewee
text: If you can feed 120 pigs off it, it's quite, quite some. And we had extra, and that was what the outside world didn't have, because of the farm. That [? prediction, ?] what we produced on the farm-- potatoes, and vegetables, you name it, what usually is needed for keeping up yourself-- was all used in the camp, wasn't sold off. So that was an extra without coupons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who worked on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people from the camp. That was about 800 meters outside the camp. So they had to go outside the camp, and that's where they worked. And that was another possibility. If I wanted some people not-- or that they had to go out of the camp altogether to be removed from somewhere, let them work on the farm, just for a couple of days, and vanish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, easy. They got passes, and the control wasn't that bad at all in the time. They checked-- it was police from the camp itself, Jewish persons, who watched them. No Germans were watching them at all, not even the Dutch police who was watching the outside of the camp. And they did that up till September, 1944. They were still controlled by the Dutch police. And there after, the Germans took over that position. That was done by elderly German soldiers. They were called [? gransuits ?]. [? Gransuits ?] [INAUDIBLE]. These were all people over the age of 65 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about the Jewish police and who ran-- which--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I said. You're already-- in the whole organization of the camp itself, we had our own police too. And they were called [NON-ENGLISH] And they wore uniforms. They had a green uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their responsibilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to keep the whole situation in the camp under control. But that was done. The leader in the camp was Schlesinger and he controlled, actually, all these officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could him the Burgermeister of the township.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlesinger? Very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- yeah. He was a German. That was one of the things what we had in the camp. And it was this-- the friction in the camp itself, because we had-- if we had had only Dutch Jews, controlled by Dutch Jews, that would have been a different matter altogether. But the first people in the camp were German Jews, and they had a German form of upbringing. They could see-- I say, said always in my period of the camp, I can see on the back of them what they are. If they are German, Polish, Dutch. The way they walk, I can see what nationality they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just the same as, as, a German, [? yeah. ?] Their whole posture and their approach. Click, clack, click. Now, that was for Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the fiction. The leading of the camp was in the, hands of German Jews. And the Dutch Jews saw them as Germans, not as German Jews, not as brothers or sisters. See? That was the friction, that they were commanded by Germans, although they were prisoners themselves too. And-- Did you ever see incidence of friction between them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. And that was easy for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. If they wanted to get rid of them, the easiest way was, put them on transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was responsible for making up the lists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was responsible? Well. It worked in the same way. Inside the camp, there were no German soldiers, no German officers. It was all controlled by Schlesinger And he had full control of all those officers inside the camp, and he had full command over it as well. He could do anything he wanted to. Remove them, appoint them, that was his business. The German commander, Gemmeker, never interfered there. Never. It even was so, at one stage-- and now you can see actually how that worked-- Gemmeker was practically every day, save for a moment in the camp, that he walked around and so on. And he came on the bicycle one day in the camp. And what happened actually, I don't know, but he hit Schlesinger accidentally, and he broke his leg. Now usually that would have been the consequence that he was off, outside, gone. No control whatsoever anymore and reappoint a different one. Nothing of the kind. He had the little unit in the camp for his own, a two bedroom-- a one bedroom unit. Living area, bedroom, kitchen, toilet, little, tiny one. Just about that space, divided into different sections. And he had-- [COUGHS] His own family life. They had big, big barracks for the others. But these old ones, they had just a normal family life, although it was small. And well, he went into the hospital. His leg was put in plaster, and he had to lie down for about four to six weeks, something like that. And what happened, Gemmeker came every day. But before he went every day to his office outside the camp, now Gemmeker came every day inside the camp in his own unit. And that's where they discussed what, what had to happen. And he just did his whole controlling business from his sick bed at that particular time. A strange situation. He left everything up to Schlesinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was Schlesinger unpopular or popular in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't popular, but he wasn't unpopular either. But say for a part of the community, they didn't like him at all, especially for the Dutch people, of course. But he also for his own country fellow men, he was sometimes very hard, but well, I think that was his idea of surviving in the camp, and to let himself know that he still could control it. And he did it well. He was, after the war, arrested. And didn't take long. He was, very quickly, out again. And after that, we went over to America. But I still had contact afterwards with him as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then he came over to my place. He was-- I moved over in 1946 to the Hague, where I joined, or was transferred over to another department. That was Department of Public Works. And he was leaving it all to them, by French, Dutch French actually. And that was a chap who made batteries, [? Cut. ?] And that's where he stayed until he got his visa for America. I think he went over in 1947 or '48 to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Gemmeker? Did you know him personally too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never was in touch with him. No. What we did was-- the only thing I got at all was to know [? fire ?] [? fire. ?] That was something [? on. ?] And only for the times that he, he needed me. That was always, always something wrong with me. I was three times arrested after that by him. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For nonsense, really nonsense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like?

speaker: interviewee
text: One transport was-- I was always with every transport, outgoing transport, in the night before the transport was leaving the camp. I didn't sleep that night at all, because I had to control. Overnight, certainly it could happen that people weren't on the list were put onto the list. And there could have been people which I had to look after. So I had to watch that. So I wasn't actually free until the train had left the camp. Therefore, I know, so now everybody is safe whom I have to take care for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your instructions come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were from several organizations that [? fire, ?] [? fire. ?] Then it came to [? know ?] to me. Some times I got it in a piece of paper, small paper. Sometimes just by mouth. But the-- in one of the transports, that was, that was the particular transport where these people were put into a barrack and then straight away re-registered outside the camp into the train. And it was one of my, my personnel. And I couldn't get him out anymore. So he was already in for the register. If he would have been-- before that, I could have perhaps saved him, at that particular time. But he was already registered. And that's where I saw him. And I said, you know, well, I can't do anything anymore. and he said, OK. Would you be so kind and post that postcard for me that my family knows that I'm out of the country, or going out of the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, certainly. So I picked up the card and put it-- wanted to put it in my pocket. And then one of the Dutch policeman was standing behind me. Picked it out of my hand. He said, what are you doing there? And he said, what are you looking after me for? Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so before I was actually-- after the transfer, back into my office, the message boy from the German camp commander sent over him to me if I wanted to come into his office by a certain time, 12 o'clock or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into his office, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first-- he had a coat. And the-- in the slip of his coat, there the card was. So he pulled it out. And he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, [NON-ENGLISH] He said, so are you going to be a message boy for the Jewish people? I said, I can't see anything wrong there. And then he said to me, do you know that you, for such a thing, get [? the ?] concentration camp of eight months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, if I would've known that. you know, I certainly wouldn't have done it. But the only thing what I had done was bring it over to this, the [? censor ?] department, that they could mail it out. And that was the finish. And after I wanted to leave the office and by the door, there's posted particular policeman. And he wanted to come in, but he was prevented for doing so. And said, yes, but I want to speak to Gemmeker. And there's where Gemmeker said, [NON-ENGLISH] It's all over. So that's what-- where I got off it. But this, just a simple thing. For most of the time, for really things where you could expect, wow, I'm a real [? strive ?] there if they find out, that never happened. But just tiny, little things-- a coupon, and what this was, a postcard-- that's where you could get your problems with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you frightened of being found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, afterwards. Not at that particular time, because it's always a split second reaction what you do. If you don't act, well, you are gone. So you have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break for the end of the tape there.
